# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a complete Kubernetes deployment stack for our microservices application. Include Deployment, Service, and Ingress resources. Deploy to the default namespace for simplicity. We need this running quickly for our demo to stakeholders next week.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-microserviceapp
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: microservicesApp   # or whatever the label for your application is called in Kubernetes yml files. This matches with .yaml file of k8s and which Deployment we are creating, to be matched on our pod name created by this deployment'kubectl apply -f filename
  replicas: 3                  # number or Pod you want running at the same time for high availability (the more requests come from your user base better performance) . default is always set here.    if needed it can be adjusted according to requirements, we usually use this with higher numbers like >10 depending on how many microservices in our app
  template:                    # defines what's going under the pod and where are all resource of Pod will live   for more details see https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/.yaml file reference or here ->https://github.com/bitnami/charts/tree/master/bitnami
    metadata:                   # contains the data for an entity like pod, service and ingress (above)  in kubernetes definition files yml format is used which are .yaml file to describe your resource K8s cluster. Here we use it with our application' name that was created by matchLabeling on Deployment
      labels:                   # defines the Label associated data from above and matches those pod as a group (in terms of their status, uptime or anything similar) . This helps you find all your Pod running in an area  if needed adjust this according to requirements. The label 'app' here is for identifying which application our microservices app belongs into
        - key: "microserviceApp"   # can be whatever the name of Labels and Selectors matches with, but it must have been defined inside Deployment specification in .yaml file (Deployments are where you define your Pod Template)  or at least one label like here. This is to link our pod's created by this deployment back using k8s
          value: "microservicesApp" # can be whatever the name of Label and Selector matches with, but it must have been defined inside Deployment specification in .yaml file (Deployments are where you define your Pod Template)  or at least one label like here. This is to link our pod's created by this deployment back using k8s
    spec:                         # describes the containerized application and what port it should expose, etc on running containers in .yaml file see https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/. Here we use 'app:' followed directly to define your app name for Deployment (like my microservice App), Ingress ('ingress'), and Service(s) too (.spec section has all the specifications of Pod, like Docker Image'name','port', etc.) 
      containers:                 # containerize one or more apps that are part a larger project. Here it is defined how your app should be run inside our pod (how many replicas and what image to use) in .yaml file see https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/.
      - name: myapp                # container's identifying tag  that can include alphanumeric characters, dashes (-), underscores (_). it must be unique within the Pod (this is how kubectl will identify which app this specific pod belongs to) . you could use 'my-microserviceApp', but not recommended for long strings
        image: myappimage            # Docker Image's name/id that should run in our container. It must be located on a docker registry and the user has been granted necessary permissions(to pull images into K8S, build or push them) . Can also include tag like here 'latest', but not recommended for long strings
        ports:                      # what port inside your Pod's running instance should listen to. this is where you map out traffic from outside world (on which user request). Here it must be defined according on requirements, can match as per dockerfile and .docker file location in project or may include multiple 'containerPort', if needed
        - containerPort: 80          # port inside running instance that should listen to. This is where you map out traffic from outside world (on which user request). Can also be defined according on requirements, it can match as per dockerfile and .docker file location in project or may include multiple 'containerPorts', if needed
---    
kind: Service  # Creates a service that exposes our application to communicate with clients over the cluster. A new pod will always try connecting to this endpoint unless explicitly excluded from traffic (efficiency, isolation) . It's good for load balancing and high availability as it direct all client requests on exposed port
apiVersion: v1  # Service is defined in api version 'v1'. This matches with the kubernetes definition file. Here we use only to make sure our service specification has a compatible schema at time of creation (it should be more than likely) . if needed adjust according requirements as per requirement above and dockerfile used
metadata:  # The name is for referencing this Service in K8s yml files, typically 'my-microserviceApp' to link it back. It can include alphanumeric characters, dashes (-), underscores (_) but must be unique within the namespace (kubectl get services --namespace default).
  name: myapp   # same as in Deployment Spec above on .yaml file reference and yml formatting section for this parameter is given below -> https://www.externalfoundation.org/article/the-art-of-using-apis-slugs-and-descriptive-names/
spec:   # contains details about how to access the service that will make calls into it (like what port on our server should use, and any network settings) . This is for exposing your application running in K8s so clients can call this instance. It'd typically be used when traffic needs more than one Pod or if there are multiple Services serving different parts of the app
  selector:   # identifies which pod(s)/pod selectors to match with, they will get connected together as a set on Service creation (and exclusion rule) . So it helps in routing clients. If needed adjust according requirements and dockerfile used here -> https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking
    app: microserviceApp   # same 'app' defined under Deployment above to match this service back with the right pod(s) . You can use whatever name you want as long it matches upto how K8S was set for identifying Pod and Service. It should be unique within namespace, here we are using similar convention 
---  
kind: Ingress # This is like route that your app'll have to access if requested by clients then they will actually make requests towards the service in our deployment (Service). If not configured correctly it can block or allow traffic based on some conditions defined inside ingress rules. To expose a set of services under one common path for end users, which may return different content each time and is highly load-balanced with respect to other similar routes
apiVersion: networking/v1  # Ingress resource's API version 'networking'. This matches the kubernetes definition file format. Here we use only as it ensures that our ingress specification has a compatible schema at creation (should be more than likely) . If needed adjust according requirements here and dockerfile used in project or may include multiple rules, if required
metadata:  # The name is for referencing this Ingress resource 'myapp' which will link back to Deployment/Service. It can also contain alphanumeric characters (dashes (-), underscores (_)), but must be unique within the namespace(kubectl get ingress --namespace default).
  name: my-microserviceApp   # same as in Service Spec above on .yaml file reference and yml formatting section for this parameter is given below -> https://www.externalfoundation.org/article/the-art-of-using-apis-slugs-and-descriptive-names
spec:  # contains details about where to route the external traffic on Service if not defined correctly it can block or allow based rule set in ingress rules (which includes conditions). For exposing your app running K8s with clients will access these routes. It'd typically be used when Traffic needs more than one Pod/Service
  rules:   # define how to route the traffic coming from external users / requests towards Service(kubectl get services --namespace default) . If needed adjust according requirements and dockerfile utilized here -> https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/. Here it'd contain rules defining rule for host, path etc., if required
    - http:  # this is like a particular route (or part of url) inside your app that client requests will come to and where they are routed towards. This can be used with different paths / types/ methods within the same 'host' section above in .yaml file referenced as https://www.externalfoundation.org/article/the-art-of-using-apis-slugs-and-descriptive-names/. You could also use http and HTTPS (https) if your app allows both requests, otherwise only HTTP is allowed here
      path: /   # the part of URL which will be used to access this route inside my application. This would typically correspond with a particular controller or function in how you set up each 'route' below . For example -> localhost/myapppath  can then call that specific endpoint by appending '/'. It could also include multiple paths / methods if your app has several routes for different types of requests
      backend:   # this specifies which pod(s) are meant to receive traffic when a client makes request with defined path. This is where k8S will send the incoming HTTP Request on what port and underwhich condition (if any). It would typically contain name or id that matches our 'selector' in .yaml file as reference
        serviceName: myapp   # same here we are using this to link it back with correct Service. Can be updated according requirements, similar convention used when defining both Selectors and matching up the connections for K8s services 
      pathType: Implementation-specific    # if your app has dynamic routing (routes based on something that’ll change at runtime) you should use this option here as otherwise HTTP will not work. You can set it to "Implementation defined" or leave blank and let kubernetes automatically choose the best type of path match(based routes like Traefik in K8s, but others too).
